source: Hayes, S.C., Strosahl, K.D., & Wilson, K.G. (1999). Acceptance and Commitment Therapy: An experiential approach to behavior change.
description: Evidence-based techniques from Acceptance and Commitment Therapy focusing on psychological flexibility through acceptance, defusion, present-moment awareness, self-as-context, values, and committed action.

strategies.name: Cognitive Defusion: Leaves on a Stream
strategies.description: Create distance from thoughts by observing them as passing mental events rather than literal truths.
steps: Find a quiet place, close your eyes, and take a few deep breaths
Imagine yourself sitting beside a gentle stream with leaves floating by
For each thought that arises, place it on a leaf and watch it float downstream
Don't try to make thoughts go away or push them downstream faster
Simply notice each thought, place it on a leaf, and let it drift
If thoughts come quickly, that's okay - put each one on its own leaf
If you get caught up in a thought, notice that and gently return to the stream
Practice for 5-10 minutes
Notice: You are the observer of thoughts, not the thoughts themselves

citations.author: Hayes, S.C., et al.
citations.title: Acceptance and Commitment Therapy: The Process and Practice of Mindful Change

strategies.contraindications: May be difficult for those with trauma-related intrusive thoughts; start with shorter sessions

strategies.name: Cognitive Defusion: 'I'm Having the Thought That...'
strategies.description: Add linguistic distance to reduce fusion with negative thoughts.
steps: Notice a distressing thought (e.g., 'I'm worthless')
Say it out loud: 'I'm worthless'
Now say: 'I'm having the thought that I'm worthless'
Now say: 'I notice I'm having the thought that I'm worthless'
Now say: 'I notice my mind is telling me that I'm worthless'
Notice how each version creates more distance
The thought becomes just words your mind produces
You don't have to believe it or act on it
Practice with various difficult thoughts throughout the day

strategies.name: Values Clarification: What Matters Most
strategies.description: Identify your core values to guide decision-making and create meaning.
steps: Consider these life domains: relationships, work/career, personal growth, health, leisure, spirituality, community
For each domain, ask: 'What kind of person do I want to be in this area?'
Write down qualities and directions (not goals): e.g., 'be loving', 'be curious', 'be honest'
Ask: 'If no one would ever know, what would I choose?'
Ask: 'On my deathbed, what would I want to have stood for?'
Narrow to your top 3-5 core values
For each value, write why it matters to you
Ask: 'Am I currently living aligned with these values?' Rate 1-10 for each
Identify one small action you can take today toward each value

citations.author: Hayes, S.C., et al.
citations.title: ACT: The Process and Practice of Mindful Change

strategies.contraindications: Can be emotionally challenging if there's large gap between values and current life

strategies.name: Committed Action: SMART Values Goals
strategies.description: Translate values into concrete, committed actions.
steps: Choose one core value to work with
Ask: 'What would someone who deeply honors this value DO?'
Create a SMART goal (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound)
Break the goal into the smallest possible first step
Make it so small you'd be embarrassed NOT to do it
Commit to when you'll do it: Date, time, location
Identify barriers that might arise and plan for them
Do the action, even if you don't feel like it (willingness)
Notice: Values are directions, not destinations - there's no 'arriving'
After action, reflect: 'How did acting on my values feel?'

citations.author: Hayes, S.C., et al.
citations.title: ACT: The Process and Practice of Mindful Change

strategies.name: Observer Self: The Chessboard Metaphor
strategies.description: Access self-as-context: the part of you that observes but is not defined by thoughts, feelings, or roles.
steps: Close your eyes and imagine a chessboard
The pieces (both black and white) are your thoughts, feelings, sensations, memories
They're constantly moving, fighting, some winning, some losing
Notice: You are not the pieces - you are the board
The board holds all the pieces but is not damaged by the battle
The board was there before the pieces and remains after
Practice this perspective: 'I am the space in which my experiences occur'
From this perspective, no single thought or feeling defines you
Ask: 'What have I been present for throughout my entire life?' (Answer: Everything)
You are the constant conscious presence, the observer
strategies.contraindications: Can be destabilizing for those with dissociative issues; use with caution

strategies.name: Willingness: Expansion Practice
strategies.description: Make room for difficult emotions and sensations rather than fighting them.
steps: Notice a difficult emotion or sensation in your body
Instead of trying to make it go away, get curious about it
Ask: 'Where exactly do I feel this in my body?'
Breathe into that area, imagine creating space around the sensation
Say to the feeling: 'I'm willing to feel you'
Not 'I want you' or 'I like you', but 'I'm willing to have you here'
Imagine the sensation as an object - what shape, color, temperature?
Practice: 'It's okay for this to be here'
Notice: Willingness reduces the struggle, which often reduces intensity
If emotion intensifies, that's okay - breathe and keep making space
Remember: You don't have to like it, just allow it

citations.author: Hayes, S.C., et al.
citations.title: ACT: The Process and Practice of Mindful Change

strategies.contraindications: Not for severe trauma symptoms without professional support

strategies.name: Present Moment: Five Senses Grounding (ACT Version)
strategies.description: Anchor awareness in the present moment through sensory contact.
steps: Pause and take a breath
Notice 3 things you can see right now - really look at details
Notice 3 things you can hear - even subtle sounds
Notice 3 things you can physically feel - texture, temperature, pressure
Say to yourself: 'Right here, right now, I am okay'
Notice: The present moment is the only place life actually happens
Past is memory, future is imagination - this moment is real
Practice throughout the day, especially when mind wanders to worry
Ask: 'Where am I?' 'What time is it?' Answer: 'Here. Now.'

citations.author: Hayes, S.C., et al.
citations.title: ACT: The Process and Practice of Mindful Change

strategies.name: Thank Your Mind
strategies.description: Develop a friendly, non-combative relationship with your thinking mind.
steps: When your mind produces a difficult thought, notice it
Instead of fighting or believing it, say: 'Thank you, mind'
Recognize: your mind is trying to protect you (even if unhelpfully)
Say: 'Thanks for trying to keep me safe, mind'
Or: 'I appreciate your input, mind'
Don't argue with the thought, just acknowledge it
Then gently redirect: 'And right now, I'm choosing to...' (values-based action)
This creates space between you and your thoughts
Your mind becomes a companion, not a dictator
Practice this throughout the day with various thoughts

meta.evidence_base: ACT is supported by over 300 RCTs showing effectiveness for anxiety, depression, chronic pain, substance abuse, and general psychological flexibility. Based on Relational Frame Theory and functional contextualism.
meta.target_populations: Applicable across diverse clinical and non-clinical populations. Particularly effective for those who struggle with experiential avoidance, cognitive fusion, and lack of values clarity.
meta.core_processes: ACT targets six core processes: Defusion, Acceptance, Present Moment, Self-as-Context, Values, and Committed Action. All strategies map to these processes.
meta.training_note: While these techniques can be practiced independently, working with an ACT-trained therapist or attending an ACT workshop enhances understanding and application of the contextual behavioral science principles.
